result:
The rain started without warning, the way good ideas often do. I was standing at the window of a borrowed apartment in Lisbon, watching the city blur into watercolor streaks of yellow streetlight and charcoal cloud. It was 2:17 a.m. and I had nothing in particular to say, which felt like the perfect reason to say everything.

There’s a peculiar freedom in being told to write freely. Most of the time we’re performing—curating ourselves for LinkedIn, for our mothers, for the version of us that lives in other people’s heads. But freedom, real freedom, is terrifying. It’s the blank page that doesn’t flinch when you admit you’re lonely at the center of a perfectly acceptable life. It’s the sentence that arrives naked and unashamed.

I’ve been thinking lately about how we’re all amateur cartographers of our own becoming. We draw maps with whatever we have: the scars on our knees from childhood, the songs that make us cry in grocery stores, the way certain people say our names like they’re tasting expensive wine. Some of us draw with blood. Some with laughter. Some with silence so complete it becomes its own kind of noise.

The city outside my window is asleep but restless. Somewhere down the street, a man is probably arguing with his wife about money. A few blocks over, a girl is writing bad poetry about a boy who doesn’t deserve it. An old woman is remembering her husband’s hands. All of them convinced, as we all are, that their particular constellation of longing is somehow unique. And the beautiful tragedy is that they’re both right and wrong. We are all the same aching animal wearing slightly different costumes.

I’ve been carrying around this theory that nostalgia isn’t about the past at all. It’s about the future we thought we’d have by now. When I miss my grandmother’s kitchen, I’m not really missing the linoleum or the way she pronounced “oregano.” I’m missing the version of myself that still believed time was generous. The girl who thought thirty was impossibly old and that love would arrive fully formed like a package delivered to the wrong address but meant for her all along.

We spend our twenties trying to become someone. Our thirties realizing that someone was mostly marketing. Our forties, if we’re lucky, beginning the slow, excruciating, magnificent work of unbecoming. Of peeling away the shoulds and the musts and the borrowed ambitions until we’re left with something small and true and a little embarrassing in its sincerity.

I want to tell you about the deer.

Last autumn I was driving through the mountains of West Virginia at dawn. The fog was so thick it felt like swimming through milk. And then, suddenly, there was this deer standing in the middle of the road. Not running. Not afraid. Just standing there like it had been waiting for me specifically. Our eyes met for what couldn’t have been more than three seconds but felt biblical. In that moment I understood something about witness. About how being truly seen by anything—animal, human, God, yourself—feels like being forgiven for crimes you didn’t know you’d committed.

The deer walked away and I pulled over and cried like an idiot. Not because I was sad. Because I was so completely, stupidly alive.

That’s what I want for all of us. More moments of being stupidly alive.

We’re so careful now. So performatively resilient. We’ve turned our wounds into brands and our healing into content. But real healing isn’t pretty. It’s ugly crying in your car to songs from 2009. It’s texting your ex at 1 a.m. and then immediately blocking them. It’s eating cereal for dinner and laughing at your own jokes and sometimes, on the best days, forgetting to be sad for whole hours at a time.

I keep a list in my phone called “Evidence.” In it are things like:

- The way my friend Sarah says “babes” when she’s excited
- How my dog dreams so hard his paws run in his sleep
- The particular blue of the sky right before it decides to rain
- That old couple slow dancing in the hardware store to music only they could hear

I add to it when the world gets too loud. When the news makes me feel like a ghost haunting my own life. When I forget that tenderness is still revolutionary.

Because that’s what we’re doing here, isn’t it? In our small, ridiculous ways. We’re practicing tenderness against all odds. We’re choosing to write the letter even though we might never send it. We’re learning the names of neighborhood cats. We’re keeping houseplants alive as an act of faith. We’re falling in love with the way light moves across someone’s face during ordinary conversation.

The rain has stopped now. Lisbon is beginning to wake up. There’s a man singing fado somewhere down the street, his voice cracking open the morning like an egg. I can hear him through the closed window and it sounds like every goodbye I never got to say properly. Like every almost that became a never. Like every version of me I had to kill so this one could live.

Writing freely feels like confession without the priest. Just you and the page and the terrifying freedom to be exactly as much of a mess as you actually are. No filter. No performance. Just the raw transmission of a human heart doing its best impression of honesty.

I don’t know what any of this means. That’s the point. Meaning is overrated anyway. What matters is the reaching. The attempt. The way we keep showing up to the blank page or the blank life or the blank relationship and saying, despite everything, “Again. Let’s try again.”

There’s a Japanese concept called wabi-sabi that I think about constantly. It’s the beauty of imperfection. The idea that something is more beautiful because it’s broken. Because it bears the marks of its journey. A cracked teacup that’s been mended with gold. A face with laugh lines. A heart that’s been shattered and superglued so many times it looks like modern art.

We are all wabi-sabi creatures stumbling through a world that wants us polished. And maybe the revolution isn’t in becoming perfect. Maybe it’s in refusing to hide our cracks. In letting the light shine through all the places we’ve been broken and remade.

The singer has stopped. The sun is coming up pink and ridiculous over the rooftops. My coffee has gone cold but I drink it anyway because wasting it feels like an insult to this particular Tuesday in this particular life that I somehow stumbled into.

I want to end with something profound but all I have is this: I’m here. You’re here. The world is unbearably beautiful and unbearably cruel, often at the same time. Keep going. Keep breaking. Keep mending. Keep writing your small true sentences on whatever blank pages life gives you.

The rest is just weather.

(Word count: 1,023)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
